title: CCCS - Batch 2: reinforcement based on real exam emphasis (July 2026)
note: Focus areas reported after passing the real exam: EKS/Kubernetes, onboarding troubleshooting, drift indication response, console filters, roles/permissions, misconfigurations/IOM.

questions.question: During AWS account registration, the CloudFormation stack fails to create. What should you check FIRST?

options.text: Whether the user deploying the stack has sufficient IAM permissions to create the required resources
Whether the Falcon sensor version is up to date
Whether the account has any EC2 instances running
Whether dark mode is enabled in the Falcon console

questions.explanation: The stack creates IAM roles and integration resources. The most common failure cause is that the user running CloudFormation lacks permission to create those resources.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Without permission to create roles/resources, the stack fails immediately.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. The sensor is not part of account registration.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Registration does not depend on having workloads.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Console theme does not affect onboarding.

questions.memory_tip: Stack failed during onboarding? Check IAM permissions of the runner first.

questions.question: A registered AWS account shows the orange Real-time visibility and detection indicator because some regions are not covered. How do you fix it?

options.text: Ensure CloudTrail management events are being captured in the missing regions and the required event rules exist there
Uninstall and reinstall the Falcon sensor on all EC2 instances
Delete the AWS account and create a new one
Disable IOAs to clear the warning

questions.explanation: Orange means active IOAs with incomplete regional coverage. The fix is to ensure CloudTrail management event capture and the event rules exist in the missing regions.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Completing CloudTrail/event rule coverage in the regions resolves it.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. The workload sensor does not feed the control plane.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Recreating the AWS account is drastic and unnecessary.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Disabling IOAs removes detection, it does not fix coverage.

questions.memory_tip: Orange = region without CloudTrail. Fix the region, not the sensor.

questions.question: An AWS account was successfully registered weeks ago, but cloud IOAs and posture assessments suddenly stopped working. What is a likely cause to investigate?

options.text: The IAM role created by the registration template was deleted or its trust policy was modified
The Falcon console password expired
Too many questions were asked to the API
The AWS region was renamed

questions.explanation: The integration depends on the IAM role created during registration. If someone deletes the role or changes the trust policy, Falcon loses access and the integration stops working.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Removed role or altered trust policy breaks the integration.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Console password does not affect the account-to-account integration.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Throttling does not permanently disable the integration.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. AWS regions are not renamed.

questions.memory_tip: Integration stopped out of nowhere? Check whether the registration's IAM role was touched.

questions.question: An auditor needs to VIEW cloud security posture findings in the Falcon console but must not change anything. Following least privilege, which role should you assign?

options.text: Falcon Administrator
CSPM Reader
Kubernetes and Containers Manager
No role; share your own credentials

questions.explanation: CSPM Reader gives read-only access to posture data, exactly what is needed for auditing, without change permissions.

option_explanations.A: Incorrect. Admin grants full access, violating least privilege.
option_explanations.B: Correct. Read-only posture access, the minimum needed.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Grants K8s management permissions the auditor does not need.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Sharing credentials violates any security policy.

questions.memory_tip: Auditor only looks: Reader.

questions.question: A platform team is responsible ONLY for managing Kubernetes protection settings in Falcon Cloud Security. Which role assignment follows least privilege?

options.text: Kubernetes and Containers Manager
Falcon Administrator for everyone on the team
CSPM Reader
No access; they should open tickets for every change

questions.explanation: The Kubernetes and Containers Manager role grants management permissions limited to the Kubernetes and containers scope, without opening the rest of the console.

option_explanations.A: Correct. K8s/containers scope, exactly the team's function.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Admin for everyone is excessive privilege.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Reader does not allow managing the settings.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Blocking the team makes operations unworkable.

questions.memory_tip: K8s-only team: K8s and containers role, not Admin.

questions.question: What is the scope of the Falcon Administrator role, and how should it be assigned?

options.text: Full access to console functionality including user management; assign it to as few people as possible
Read-only access to detections; assign it to everyone
Access limited to reports; assign it to service accounts only
Access only to Kubernetes settings

questions.explanation: Falcon Administrator accesses practically the entire console, including user management. That is why it should be assigned to as few people as possible.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Full access = minimal, controlled assignment.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Admin is the opposite of read-only, and never for everyone.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. It is not limited to reports.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. K8s scope belongs to a different role.

questions.memory_tip: Falcon Admin: full power, very few people.

questions.question: Several analysts each need a slightly different mix of permissions in the Falcon console. What is the least-privilege approach?

options.text: Give everyone Falcon Administrator to avoid friction
Start from predefined roles and create custom roles containing only the permissions each function needs
Share one generic account among all analysts
Grant all roles to all analysts and remove later if there are complaints

questions.explanation: The correct model is to start from predefined roles and create custom roles with only the permissions each function needs.

option_explanations.A: Incorrect. Blanket Admin is the anti-pattern of privilege.
option_explanations.B: Correct. Custom roles with the minimum needed per function.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. A shared account eliminates traceability.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Granting everything and removing later inverts the principle.

questions.memory_tip: Least privilege in the console: custom role per function.

questions.question: Your team must prioritize critical misconfigurations in a specific AWS account among thousands of IOM findings. Which filter combination gets you there fastest?

options.text: Severity (Critical) + Cloud provider/Account
Alphabetical order of resource names
Date created, oldest first
Randomly sampling findings

questions.explanation: Filtering by critical severity and narrowing to the desired provider/account reduces the volume to what matters right now.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Severity + account scope = immediate prioritization.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Alphabetical order does not prioritize risk.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Age alone does not indicate criticality.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Sampling ignores actual risk.

questions.memory_tip: Prioritize IOM: filter by severity + account.

questions.question: After remediating a misconfiguration in the cloud console, how do you confirm the IOM finding is resolved in Falcon Cloud Security?

options.text: The finding closes after the next assessment re-evaluates the resource and no longer detects the issue
You must manually delete the finding from the database
IOM findings never close once created
You must re-register the cloud account

questions.explanation: Posture assessments are continuous: when the next assessment checks the fixed resource, the finding closes automatically.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Re-assessment confirms the fix and closes the finding.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. There is no manual database deletion.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Findings close once the issue disappears.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Re-registering is not needed to update findings.

questions.memory_tip: Fixed it? The next assessment closes the IOM on its own.

questions.question: A specific IOM keeps firing in a development account where the team has formally accepted the risk. What is the appropriate way to reduce this noise?

options.text: Create a suppression/exclusion for that finding scoped to the dev account
Disable the entire CSPM policy for all accounts
Unregister the development account
Lower every rule's severity to informational

questions.explanation: Scoped suppressions silence an accepted finding in a specific environment without losing coverage across the rest of the organization.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Scoped suppression: silences only what was accepted, where it was accepted.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Disabling the entire policy removes coverage for everyone.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Losing visibility into dev is not managing risk.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Downgrading everything distorts prioritization.

questions.memory_tip: Accepted risk in one environment: scoped suppression, never disable the policy.

questions.question: Leadership asks how many running containers in the environment are NOT protected by a Falcon sensor. Where do you find this quickly?

options.text: In the Kubernetes and containers inventory, filtering by protection status
By connecting to each cluster with kubectl and counting pods manually
In the cloud provider's billing dashboard
This information cannot be obtained

questions.explanation: The Kubernetes and containers inventory lets you filter workloads by protection status, quickly showing the coverage gap.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Inventory + protection filter = immediate coverage view.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Manual counting does not scale and does not reflect protection.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Billing does not know what is protected.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. The visibility exists and is one of the platform's strengths.

questions.memory_tip: Container coverage: K8s inventory, protection filter.

questions.question: What condition generates a container drift indicator?

options.text: A new binary is created and executed inside a running container that was not part of the original image
A container restarts due to a health check failure
An image receives a new tag in the registry
A pod is rescheduled to another node

questions.explanation: Drift is a break in immutability: a new binary created and executed in the container at runtime, something that did not exist in the original image.

option_explanations.A: Correct. New binary created and executed = drift.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Restart from a health check failure is normal operation.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. A new tag in the registry does not change running containers.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Rescheduling is orchestrator behavior.

questions.memory_tip: Drift = new binary running that was not shipped in the image.

questions.question: A drift indicator fired because a developer hotfixed a running container by copying a new binary into it. The change itself is legitimate. What is the correct long-term response?

options.text: Rebuild the container image to include the change and redeploy, keeping containers immutable
Keep patching running containers manually going forward
Disable drift detection to stop the alerts
Delete the drift indicator and do nothing else

questions.explanation: Even legitimate changes should go through the image: rebuild, test, and redeploy preserve immutability and keep drift detection useful.

option_explanations.A: Correct. The change goes into the image, the container becomes immutable again.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Manual patching becomes a permanent drift practice.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Disabling detection hides real future attacks.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Ignoring it keeps the container diverging from the image.

questions.memory_tip: Legitimate drift: put the change into the image (rebuild + redeploy).

questions.question: A drift indicator shows an unknown binary executing in a production container, and no deployment or hotfix explains it. What should you do?

options.text: Investigate the container's associated events (process, file, network) and contain or stop the workload if malicious activity is confirmed
Wait a week to see if more indicators appear
Delete the indicator because production is sensitive
Reboot every node in the cluster immediately

questions.explanation: Unexplained drift in production is a possible compromise: investigate the container's events (which are tied to the container, not the host) and contain it if malicious activity is confirmed.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Investigate the container's events and contain if malicious.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Waiting gives the adversary time.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Deleting the indicator destroys evidence.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Rebooting everything destroys evidence and brings down production without a diagnosis.

questions.memory_tip: Unexplained drift: investigate the container's events, contain if needed.

questions.question: After a scheduled credential rotation, a previously Operational registry connection starts failing assessments. What fixes it?

options.text: Update the stored credentials in the registry connection settings
Delete all images from the registry
Create a new Falcon tenant
Disable image assessments permanently

questions.explanation: The connection uses stored credentials. After a rotation, simply updating them in the connection settings restores the status to Operational.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Rotated credential = update the connection.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. The images are not the cause of the authentication issue.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. A new tenant is disproportionate.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Disabling assessments removes coverage.

questions.memory_tip: Rotated a credential? Update the registry connection.

questions.question: How does the Falcon Container sensor get added to application pods automatically at scale?

options.text: Through a sensor injector using a Kubernetes mutating admission webhook that adds the sidecar when pods are created
An administrator edits every pod manifest by hand
The cloud provider adds it during billing processing
Developers must import a library in the application code

questions.explanation: The sensor injector uses a mutating admission webhook: when a pod is created, the Falcon Container sensor sidecar is automatically injected into the spec.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Automatic injection via mutating webhook at pod creation.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Manual editing does not scale and creates gaps.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. The provider does not inject sensors.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. There is no dependency on application code.

questions.memory_tip: Sidecar at scale: injector + mutating webhook at pod creation.

questions.question: Why can't the Falcon sensor for Linux (DaemonSet) protect pods running on AWS Fargate?

options.text: Fargate gives no access to the underlying worker node or kernel, and privileged containers are not allowed
Fargate only runs Windows containers
DaemonSets are not supported in any AWS service
The sensor license excludes AWS

questions.explanation: Fargate is serverless: there is no accessible node or exposed kernel, and privileged containers are blocked. That is why protection comes from the Container sensor as a sidecar.

option_explanations.A: Correct. No node, no kernel, no privilege: no DaemonSet.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Fargate runs Linux containers.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. DaemonSets work in EKS with EC2 nodes.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Licensing is not the limitation.

questions.memory_tip: Fargate hides the node: DaemonSet has nowhere to live.

questions.question: What coverage does ONE Falcon sensor for Linux deployed via DaemonSet provide on a Kubernetes node?

options.text: It protects the node and ALL containers/pods running on that node
It protects only one pod chosen at random
It protects only the kube-system namespace
It protects other nodes in the cluster as well

questions.explanation: The node sensor operates at the host kernel level: one sensor per node covers the node and all pods running on it. The sidecar, by contrast, covers one pod per injection.

option_explanations.A: Correct. One sensor per node = every pod on that node.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Coverage is total on the node, not random.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. There is no namespace limitation.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Each node needs its own sensor (hence DaemonSet).

questions.memory_tip: Node with a sensor: everything running on it is covered.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Falcon Operator in Kubernetes environments?

options.text: To automate the deployment and lifecycle management of Falcon sensors and components in the cluster, including scenarios like EKS Fargate
To replace the Kubernetes scheduler
To convert containers into virtual machines
To manage cloud billing budgets

questions.explanation: The Falcon Operator automates the deployment and lifecycle of Falcon components in the cluster (node sensor, container sensor, admission controller), including support for scenarios like EKS Fargate.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Operator = automated deployment and lifecycle in the cluster.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. The scheduler remains Kubernetes' own.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. There is no conversion of containers into VMs.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Billing is not the Operator's scope.

questions.memory_tip: Operator: installs and maintains Falcon components in the cluster.

questions.question: You deployed the Falcon sensor DaemonSet on an EKS cluster, but the Kubernetes inventory still shows some pods as unprotected. Investigation shows those pods run on a Fargate profile. What is the fix?

options.text: Deploy the Falcon Container sensor so it is injected as a sidecar into the Fargate pods
Increase the DaemonSet replica count
Move the DaemonSet to the kube-system namespace
Restart the Fargate pods until the DaemonSet reaches them

questions.explanation: Pods on Fargate have no accessible node, so the DaemonSet will never cover them. Coverage comes from the Container sensor injected as a sidecar into those pods.

option_explanations.A: Correct. Fargate = sidecar. The gap closes with the Container sensor.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. DaemonSet does not use configurable per-pod replicas, and Fargate remains inaccessible.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Namespace does not change the DaemonSet's reach.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Restarting does not make the DaemonSet reach Fargate.

questions.memory_tip: Unprotected pod on Fargate: inject the sidecar, do not touch the DaemonSet.
